content.full_text:
DARRYL R. WOLD
ATTORNEY AT LAW
3151 AIRWAY AVENUE, SUITE M-1
COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92626
(714) 641-4942    FAX (714) 546-1003


January 21, 1991


Jonathan Rothman, Esq.
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J. Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: Request For Written Advice

Dear Jonathan:

     This letter follows up on our telephone conversation on December 20, 1990, and requests written advice from the Commission pursuant to Government Code §83114(b).

     I am authorized to make this request on behalf of my clients: Brad Gates, an incumbent officeholder as Sheriff of Orange County; Friends of Brad Gates, his controlled campaign committee (the "Committee"); and David R. White II, CPA, the Committee's treasurer.

     In our telephone conversation of December 20, I requested advice concerning the application of the provisions of Chapter 5, Article 8 of the Political Reform Act, and in particular the provisions concerning the use of campaign funds to make gifts, to a particular factual situation. (These provisions have now been repealed and re-enacted under different numbers, but apparently without substantive change that would affect the advice you gave me in our telephone conversation.)

     The fact situation is this: The Committee wishes to hold a fund-raising event, probably a reception with hosted cocktails and hors d'oeuvres. It will ask for contributions of $1,000 per person to attend the event. The solicitation to potential contributors will publicize the Committee's intention to give a gift to each contributor of $1,000 for this event, and will describe the gift in some detail. The Committee feels that the nature of the gift will provide at least some incentive to potential contributors to make the contribution of $1,000 who cannot attend the event. (Contributors of $1,000 who cannot attend the event would nonetheless be given the gift.)

Jonathan Rothman, Esq.
January 21, 1991
Page Two


The gift will be a western style belt buckle, made of solid
sterling silver and featuring a bust of the likeness of John
Wayne designed and created for the buckle by an artist
commissioned for this purpose. John Wayne's likeness will be used
by permission of Wayne Enterprises, holders of exclusive rights
to the use of his likeness, without charge to the Committee.
The buckles will be produced in a numbered limited edition, in a
quantity needed for this event. The cost to the Committee of the
buckles, including for design, materials, production, and
packaging, will be approximately $230 per buckle. (This figure
does not include the value of the permission to use the likeness
of John Wayne, which will be donated to the Committee and which
is at present undetermined.)

The Committee does not anticipate personalizing the buckles.
The Committee has not made any determination as to the value of
the individual buckles. For those reasons, the Committee does
not anticipate that the buckles would be excluded from the
definition of gifts by §82028(b)(6).

You suggested in our telephone conversation that it appeared
that the gift of the buckles would be directly tied to a
political purpose, and would therefor not be prohibited by the
provisions of the Act. You also recommended, however, that the
Committee obtain written advice on this issue before proceeding.
This letter therefor requests written advice whether the gift of
the buckles as proposed in this letter would be prohibited by the
Act.

If you need any further information before responding,
please let me know. Thank you very much for your assistance in
this matter.

Sincerely,

[signature]
DARRYL Q. WOLD

DRW/km

ROTHMAN.LTR

sections.question: This letter therefor requests srritten advice whether the gift of the buckles as proposed in this letter would be prohibited by the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Are campaign committee gifts of sterling silver belt buckles featuring John Wayne's likeness, costing approximately $230 per buckle and given to $1,000 contributors, prohibited under the Political Reform Act's provisions concerning use of campaign funds for gifts?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. No formal CONCLUSION or QUESTION section headers present. The question is identified from the final paragraph. The letter references a prior telephone conversation and requests written advice pursuant to Government Code §83114(b). Document contains detailed factual scenario about proposed campaign gift.

embedding.summary: Attorney Darryl Wold requests written advice from the FPPC on behalf of Brad Gates (Orange County Sheriff), his campaign committee, and the committee treasurer regarding whether the committee may gift custom sterling silver belt buckles (costing ~$230 each and featuring John Wayne's likeness) to contributors of $1,000 to a fundraising event.
